22: A type: 'There I will meet with you, and from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim that are on the ark of the testimony, I will speak with you.' The mercy seat (kapporet, from kipper, to atone/cover) is the lid of the ark, sprinkled with blood on Yom Kippur. Paul in Romans 3:25 calls Christ a hilasterion — the LXX word for the mercy seat. Christ is both the priest who offers the sacrifice and the mercy seat on which the blood is placed; he is both offerer and the place of offering, the one who makes atonement and the locus where God and humanity meet. The tabernacle's most restricted and holy object — accessible only once a year, only by the high priest, only with blood — is fulfilled in Christ who provides permanent access to God.
1: A type: YHWH speaks to Moses concerning the tabernacle — the entire sanctuary is divinely ordered, not a human invention. Jesus institutes the true mode of worship: “in spirit and truth” (John 4:24); every pattern of legitimate worship originates in the Father’s initiative. The church is built not by human religious imagination but by divine design (Matthew 16:18).
2: A type: nedabah (nedābāh), freewill offering “from everyone whose heart moves him” — grace transforms obligation into delight. Paul echoes the principle: “each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver” (2 Corinthians 9:7). The new covenant motivation is gratitude flowing from the gospel (2 Corinthians 8:7–9), not legal coercion.
3: A type: the materials (gold, silver, bronze, fine fabrics, acacia wood, oil, spices, gems) were largely the wealth Israel carried out of Egypt (Exodus 12:35–36). Augustine’s principle: plundering the Egyptians prefigures consecrating the world’s goods to Christ’s purposes. All created wealth finds its proper telos in the worship and dwelling of God with his people (1 Corinthians 10:26; Revelation 21:24–26).
4: A type: the colored yarns — blue (tekhelet), purple (argaman), and scarlet (tolaʿat shani) — are the liturgical palette of the tabernacle and later the high priest’s garments. Blue signals the heavenly, scarlet the earthly and sacrificial; together they speak of the incarnate mediator who joins heaven and earth. The same colors appear on the veil and the high priestly robe, all pointing to the one who bridges the divine and human realms.
5: A type: tanned rams’ skins dyed red and goat skins — animal coverings for the sanctuary’s outer layer. Animal skins recall the first covering of human shame after the fall (Genesis 3:21); the tabernacle is covered in the same material, signaling that atonement-provision wraps the entire sanctuary from outside. Christ’s incarnation and sacrifice is the true covering of human nakedness before God (Galatians 3:27).
6: A type: acacia wood (ʿăṣê šiṭṭîm) — a desert hardwood, resistant to decay and insects, the structural material of the tabernacle’s frames, poles, and furniture. Its incorruptibility makes it a fitting type of Christ’s humanity — fully human yet untouched by moral corruption (Hebrews 4:15; Acts 2:27 “you will not let your Holy One see corruption”). All overlaid with gold: the human (acacia) clothed in the divine (gold).
7: A type: the onyx stones and stones for setting in the ephod and breastpiece — the names of the twelve tribes engraved on the high priest’s stones, carried before YHWH. Christ as the great high priest bears his people before the Father: “he holds his priesthood permanently because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24); “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25).
8: A fulfillment: “Let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell in their midst.” The entire purpose of the tabernacle is stated here. John 1:14 uses the cognate tabernacle verb: the Word “tabernacled” (eskēnōsen) among us — the Incarnation is the direct fulfillment of the tabernacle’s stated purpose. The climax of Revelation 21:3 echoes the same language: “Behold, the dwelling (skēnē) of God is with man. He will dwell with them.”
9: A type: tabnit (tabnît, pattern/model) — “exactly as I show you the pattern of the tabernacle and of all its furniture, so you shall make it.” Hebrews 8:5 quotes this verse directly to establish that the Levitical priests “serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things.” The earthly tabernacle was never ultimate; it was always pointing beyond itself to the heavenly sanctuary where Christ now ministers as high priest (Hebrews 8:1–2; Exodus 9:24).
10: A type: the ark of the covenant — 2.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 cubits; the central piece of tabernacle furniture, the throne-seat of YHWH’s presence. Its three contents (jar of manna, Aaron’s rod, tablets) are the covenant’s three signs; Hebrews 9:4 lists all three. Christ fulfills all: he is the true bread from heaven (John 6:35), the true high priest (Hebrews 4:14), and the living law written on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3).
11: A type: gold inside and out — purity throughout, not merely exterior. The ark’s total golden overlay speaks of divine holiness intrinsic and complete. Christ’s holiness was not acquired or performed but intrinsic: “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26). Unlike Israel’s priests who needed daily purification, the Son was entirely golden — pure throughout.
12: A type: four golden rings on the four feet of the ark — the carrying apparatus; the ark was built for movement. YHWH’s presence accompanies his people on their journey; he is not a static, territorial deity. The Spirit-indwelt church is the mobile dwelling of God in the world (1 Corinthians 3:16; 2 Corinthians 6:16), accompanying believers throughout their pilgrimage.
13: A type: poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold for carrying the ark — the ark was moved by levitical carriers, never by ox-cart (2 Samuel 6:6–7 shows the cost of neglecting this). The means of carrying God’s presence must conform to his own prescribed pattern; the church’s proclamation of Christ must follow the manner Christ himself ordained (1 Corinthians 2:1–5; 2 Corinthians 4:5–7).
14: A type: “The poles shall remain in the rings of the ark; they shall not be taken from it.” The permanently installed poles signal readiness to move — the ark is never finally “settled” until it reaches its permanent home. Christ has ascended and is now at rest at the Father’s right hand (Hebrews 1:3); the church, however, is still on pilgrimage, carrying the gospel through the wilderness of this age (Hebrews 13:14).
15: A type: “Into the ark you shall put the testimony that I shall give you” — the tablets of the Decalogue are placed inside the ark. The law is at the heart of YHWH’s throne. Colossians 2:13–14: God forgave “by canceling the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands... nailing it to the cross.” The law inside the ark, covered by the blood-sprinkled mercy seat, pictures judgment covered by atonement — fulfilled in Christ.
16: A type: the testimony (ʿēdût) placed inside the ark — the tablets of the law are the covenant stipulations that Israel failed to keep. The ark containing the law becomes the ark of the covenant — the sealed covenant document. Christ “did not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets... but to fulfill” (Matthew 5:17); his perfect keeping of the law is credited to all who are in him (Romans 5:19; Philippians 3:9).
17: A fulfillment: kapporet (kappōret, mercy seat/atonement cover) — the lid of the ark, sprinkled with the high priest’s blood on Yom Kippur. The LXX renders kappōret as hilastērion. Paul uses hilastērion in Romans 3:25: God “put forward [Christ] as a propitiation (hilastērion) by his blood, to be received by faith.” Christ is the true mercy seat — the place where divine justice and divine mercy meet, where the blood is applied, where God speaks (v22).
18: A type: two cherubim of beaten gold on the two ends of the mercy seat — the cherubim guard holy space (Genesis 3:24) and form YHWH’s throne (Psalms 80:1; Exodus 99:1). Their outstretched wings sheltering the mercy seat picture the divine presence enthroned above atonement. John 20:12 describes two angels at the empty tomb “one at the head and one at the feet where the body of Jesus had lain” — a possible echo of the two cherubim flanking the ark’s atonement cover.
19: A type: the two cherubim of one piece with the mercy seat, facing each other, looking down toward the mercy seat — the cherubim’s gaze is toward the place of atonement. 1 Peter 1:12 says the angels “long to look into” the things of salvation — the kerubim’s downward gaze toward the blood-covered kapporet prefigures the angels’ fascination with the mystery of redemption accomplished in Christ.
20: A type: “The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat with their wings, their faces one to another” — the cherubim’s overshadowing wings form the canopy of YHWH’s throne. Luke 1:35: the Holy Spirit “will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you” — the same overshadowing presence that rested on the ark now rested on Mary, incarnating the Son who is YHWH’s true dwelling.
21: A type: “Put the mercy seat on top of the ark, and in the ark you shall put the testimony.” The architecture of grace: the blood-covered kappōret above the law-containing ark means that when YHWH looks at the law’s demands, he looks through the lens of atonement. Christ’s blood covers the law’s condemnation (Romans 8:1 “no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus”; Colossians 2:13–14).
23: A type: the table of the Bread of the Presence — acacia wood overlaid with gold, set in the holy place. The table is the place of the ongoing covenant meal before YHWH; it prefigures the Lord’s Supper as the continuing covenant meal of the new community. Revelation 3:20: “I will come in to him and eat with him, and he with me” — Christ as the host of the eschatological feast (Luke 22:30; Revelation 19:9).
24: A type: the table overlaid with pure gold and crowned with a gold molding — the table for God’s bread is entirely golden, signifying its holy and divine character. The Lord’s Supper is not a merely human meal but a sacred participation in Christ’s body and blood (1 Corinthians 10:16 “a participation in the blood of Christ... a participation in the body of Christ”).
25: A type: the border (frame) of a handbreadth around the table with a golden molding on its border — the structural rim; the table is bounded, a defined sacred space. The communion meal has defined boundaries: examined participation (1 Corinthians 11:28), fencing of the table as a pastoral practice to protect the holy from desecration (1 Corinthians 11:29–30).
26: A type: four golden rings at the four corners of the table for carrying poles — the table of the Bread of the Presence is portable, accompanying Israel through the wilderness. The provision of Christ for his people is not stationary; he feeds his church throughout the entire journey: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35).
27: A type: rings close to the border for the poles — the carrying apparatus securely attached. The guarantee that the table of provision will not be left behind; Christ’s promise to his church: “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). The table of his presence travels with the people through every wilderness.
28: A type: poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold for carrying the table — the same acacia-and-gold construction as the ark’s poles; wood (the human, corruptible realm) overlaid with gold (the divine, eternal realm). The union of materials reflects the incarnation: the eternal Son in mortal flesh, carrying provision for his people through the wilderness of this age.
29: A type: the plates, dishes, flagons, and bowls of pure gold for pouring drink offerings — the service vessels for the Bread of the Presence; all gold, all sacred. The constant service at the table of the Presence prefigures Christ’s perpetual intercession before the Father (Hebrews 7:25 “he always lives to make intercession for them”) and his “flesh... true food and... blood... true drink” (John 6:55).
30: A type: lehem happanim (leḥem happānîm), bread of the Face/Presence — twelve loaves before YHWH always, representing the twelve tribes in continuous covenant relationship. Christ is the true lehem happanim: “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35); he “now appears in the presence of God on our behalf” (Hebrews 9:24 — literally “now to appear before the face of God”). The twelve loaves always before the divine face are fulfilled in Christ perpetually present before the Father as our advocate.
31: A type: the golden menorah (menôrāh) — beaten from a single talent of pure gold, its shaft, branches, cups, calyxes, and petals all of one piece. John 1:4–5: “In him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness.” The single-piece menorah prefigures Christ as the one unified source of all spiritual light; its branches draw their light from the central shaft as the church draws its light from Christ (John 15:5).
32: A type: six branches from the central shaft, three on each side — the lampstand’s organic branching form like a flowering almond tree (shāqēd). Revelation 22:2 “the tree of life... bearing twelve kinds of fruit” echoes the menorah’s life-bearing form. Zechariah 4:2–6 interprets a seven-branched lampstand as the Spirit empowering Zerubbabel: “not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit” — the church’s light is Spirit-fed, not self-generated.
33: A type: three cups shaped like almond blossoms on each of the six branches — the almond (shāqēd) is the “watching tree,” first to bloom in spring, symbolizing YHWH’s watchful readiness (Jeremiah 1:11–12). The menorah’s almond-blossom form proclaims that the light of God’s presence is the fruit of his watchful, active care; Christ as the light of the world is the fulfillment of YHWH’s vigilant faithfulness (Revelation 2:1 Christ “walks among the seven golden lampstands”).
34: A type: four cups shaped like almond blossoms on the central shaft — the shaft has more cups than the branches; the central source has greater fullness than the branches it feeds. Christ has the Spirit “without measure” (John 3:34); from his fullness the church receives “grace upon grace” (John 1:16). The branches depend on the shaft’s superior supply.
35: A type: a calyx of one piece under each pair of branches — the structural unity of shaft and branches; the branches grow from the same stock. John 15:1–5: “I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.” The menorah’s organic unity is a type of the vine-and-branches union Christ describes.
36: A type: “Their calyxes and their branches shall be of one piece with it, the whole of it a single piece of hammered pure gold.” The entire menorah — all seven branches, all their cups and blossoms — is one piece of beaten gold, not assembled from separate parts. The unity of the church in Christ: one body (Ephesians 4:4), one Spirit, one baptism; “that they may all be one” (John 17:21). The unity is not constructed but intrinsic.
37: A type: “You shall make seven lamps for it” — seven = completeness and fullness; the seven lamps give light across the entire sanctuary. Revelation 1:20: “the seven lampstands are the seven churches” — the churches collectively embody the fullness of Christ’s light in the world. Matthew 5:14: “you are the light of the world” — Christ’s people are the ongoing lampstand carrying his light into every darkness.
38: A type: the snuffers and trays of pure gold — the tools for tending and trimming the wicks; priestly maintenance work. The church’s witness (the light) requires active tending: spiritual discipline (1 Timothy 4:7), correction of error (2 Timothy 4:2), the Spirit’s ongoing work (Revelation 2:5 “I will come to you and remove your lampstand from its place, unless you repent”).
39: A type: “It shall be made, along with all these utensils, out of a talent of pure gold.” One talent (ca. 75 lbs) of pure gold for the entire lampstand system — the costliest single-item investment of the tabernacle. The church’s light is not cheap; it was purchased at infinite cost. 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought with a price.” 1 Peter 1:18–19: “not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ.”
40: A type: “See that you make them after the pattern (tabnît) for them, which is being shown you on the mountain.” The repetition of the pattern-command frames the entire tabernacle instruction section (25:9 and Exodus 25:40). Hebrews 8:5 quotes this verse: the Levitical system is “a copy and shadow of the heavenly things.” Christ fulfills every element of the divine pattern fully and finally, rendering the shadow-system complete and obsolete (Hebrews 8:13; Exodus 10:1).
